blocks.content: They didn't talk much on the ride home. Certain mornings needed time before they could become stories. The streets went past — the same streets, the same houses, the same Millhaven — and everything looked exactly like it always did, which felt strange after an hour of nothing looking like anything at all.

Eli broke first. Somewhere around the third block he started laughing, and then couldn't stop, and Savannah caught it from him, and by the fourth block even Kit was gone — four kids on bikes in the middle of an empty street, losing it completely, the morning finally allowed to be funny now that it was over.
She was smiling. But it was the kind with something underneath it. Kit caught it twice and looked at the road both times.

They split at the corner — Eli east, Savannah south — and then it was just Kit and Daisy heading north on quiet streets, riding slow, no particular reason to hurry. Someone's sprinkler swept across the sidewalk in a steady arc. They rode through the wet patch and neither of them said anything about it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I told you I wasn't a say-it person. Didn't say anything about a do-it person.
A pause. A full block of it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Stick my foot through a rotten stair?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Make me stand outside a fence not knowing if you're coming.
He heard the difference between what she said and what she meant. It was a small difference and also not a small difference at all.
He didn't have anything to say to that. They were almost to Magnolia Street. The corner was right there.
